hints.perfect passive participle: Perfect passive participle would be 'acceptus' — 'Accipiens' with -ns is present active
hints.future active participle: Future active participle would be 'accepturus' — 'Accipiens' is present
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'accipiendus' — 'Accipiens' is the present active participle

hints.to accept an invitation: While related in English, the Latin 'accipere' broadly means 'to receive/take'

hints.capio, capere, cepi, captum: These are the parts of 'capere' (to seize/take) — 'accipere' is its compound ad + capere
hints.rapio, rapere, rapui, raptum: These are the parts of 'rapere' (to snatch)
hints.recipio, recipere, recepi, receptum: These are the parts of 'recipere' (to take back/receive back)

hints.nominative: While 'panes' looks identical in nominative plural, here it is the object of 'accipiens'
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'panibus' — 'panes' is accusative plural
hints.vocative: Vocative would be used for direct address — 'panes' is accusative object

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'panis' has gen. -is (3rd decl.)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'panis' has gen. -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'panis' has gen. -is

hints.nominative: While 'pisces' looks identical in nominative, it is parallel to 'panes' as object of 'accipiens'
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'piscibus' — 'pisces' is accusative plural
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'piscis' — 'pisces' is accusative plural

hints.nominative: Nominative masculine plural of 'duo' would be 'duo' — 'duos' is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'duobus' — 'duos' is accusative, modifying 'pisces'

hints.yes, 1st/2nd declension: Cardinal numbers from 4 onward are indeclinable in Latin
hints.yes, 3rd declension: Cardinal numbers from 4 onward are indeclinable — only unus, duo, tres decline

translation: Taking five loaves and two fish
